subsystem: newsbeuter/newsboat podcast queue loader — queueloader::get_filename() builds the on-disk download filename for a podcast enclosure. It takes an enclosure URL/name, derives a basename, and prefixes it with the configured download directory, producing the path string that podbeuter later hands to a downloader and, ultimately, to a shell command line.
provenance: Identified by the class/method name 'queueloader::get_filename', the newsbeuter-specific macro 'NEWSBEUTER_PATH_SEP', the controller accessor 'ctrl->get_dlpath()', the project utility 'utils::replace_all', and the sample tag '/* sample: primevul_train_2865_fixed */' plus the file name 'eval_0073_cwe78.cpp'. This is the newsbeuter podcast (podbeuter) subsystem; the single-quote-to-%27 substitution is the documented shell-injection hardening for that code path.
trust_boundary: The 'str' argument is attacker-controllable: it originates from a podcast <enclosure> URL / media name inside a remotely fetched RSS/Atom feed, which the feed publisher fully controls. The derived filename is later embedded in a shell command that invokes the external downloader / media player, so 'str' crosses from remote wire data into a command line. 'ctrl->get_dlpath()' is locally configured (download-dir), lower trust but still user-supplied and NOT escaped by this function.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Method on the queueloader class. It receives 'str' by const reference — the enclosure URL or media name taken from a podcast feed item — and returns a std::string that will be used as the local download path for that enclosure. Because feeds are fetched from the network and the publisher controls the enclosure URL, 'str' is fully attacker-influenced input at this boundary.

Initializes the result 'fn' with the configured download directory obtained from the controller. This is the path prefix onto which the derived filename will be appended. Its value comes from user configuration (the 'download-path'/'download-dir' setting) and is copied verbatim without any escaping in this function.

Ensures the download directory ends with a path separator so the appended filename forms a valid path. It indexes the last character of 'fn' via 'fn[fn.length()-1]' and compares it to the first character of the NEWSBEUTER_PATH_SEP macro ("/" on POSIX). If the directory does not already end in the separator, the separator string is appended. This assumes 'fn' is non-empty.

Declares a fixed 1024-byte stack buffer and copies the untrusted 'str' into it using snprintf with a string-literal "%s" format. snprintf bounds the write to sizeof(buf) and always NUL-terminates, so this copy is size-safe and truncates 'str' at 1023 bytes. Importantly the format argument is the literal "%s", not 'str' itself, so this is NOT a format-string bug — user data is confined to the variadic argument. The copy exists because the next call, basename(), needs a mutable C string.

Calls POSIX basename() on the buffer to strip the directory portion of the URL/name and keep only the final path component (the intended filename). 'base' points either into 'buf' or into internal/static storage depending on the basename() variant; it remains valid for the rest of this function. basename() collapses trailing slashes and returns "." for empty or all-slash input.

Fallback path when basename() yields nothing usable (NULL or empty). It formats the current local time into a fixed 128-byte buffer using the constant strftime format "%Y-%b-%d-%H%M%S.unknown" and appends that generated name to 'fn'. The format string is constant and the produced text (roughly "2026-Jul-06-153000.unknown") is far shorter than 128 bytes, so lbuf cannot overflow. This branch guarantees a filename even when the enclosure name is degenerate.

Normal path: append the derived basename to the download directory, but first pass it through utils::replace_all(base, "'", "%27") which replaces every single-quote character with the percent-escape "%27". This is the security-relevant mitigation: the resulting filename is destined for a shell command in which the argument is wrapped in single quotes, and removing literal single quotes prevents the attacker from closing that quote and injecting shell syntax. This is precisely the hardening added in the 'fixed' revision of this sample.

Returns the assembled path 'fn' = download-dir + separator + (escaped basename | timestamp). The caller (podbeuter's download queue) stores this as the target path and later composes a shell command line around it to fetch/play the enclosure, which is why the single-quote escaping above matters.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: If ctrl->get_dlpath() returns an empty string, 'fn.length()' is 0 and 'fn.length()-1' underflows the unsigned size_t to SIZE_MAX (18446744073709551615). 'fn[SIZE_MAX]' indexes std::string::operator[] wildly out of bounds — this is undefined behavior (an out-of-bounds read), which in practice reads arbitrary memory or crashes. std::string::operator[] only defines index == size() (returning the NUL) in C++11; any larger index, and certainly SIZE_MAX, is UB with no bounds check. The invariant the code must uphold is 'fn is non-empty before indexing its last character' — it should test fn.empty() first (or use fn.back() only after an emptiness check). An empty/unset download-path configuration is enough to reach this; it is a reliability/OOB-read defect, not attacker-remote by itself, but it is a concrete latent bug on line 5.

This line is the sole barrier against OS command injection (CWE-78): the returned path is later interpolated into a shell command that podbeuter runs to download/play the enclosure. The mitigation only neutralizes the single-quote character, which is correct and sufficient ONLY IF the downstream command wraps this argument in single quotes (inside a POSIX single-quoted string every other metacharacter — $, `, ;, |, &, newline, whitespace — is literal). The safety of this whole function therefore depends on an unstated external contract: the consumer must single-quote the argument. If any call site uses double quotes, no quotes, or reuses the string in a context where '%27' is later decoded back to a quote, the attacker regains injection via a crafted enclosure URL. Additionally, only 'base' is escaped here — the 'fn' prefix from ctrl->get_dlpath() (line 3) is concatenated WITHOUT escaping, so a download-path containing a single quote is not neutralized. Invariant: every attacker- or user-influenced component placed into the shell command must be escaped for the exact quoting context the command uses, and that context must actually be single-quoted.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
snprintf(buf, sizeof(buf), "%s", str.c_str());
	char * base = basename(buf);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The 1024-byte snprintf copy silently truncates any enclosure name longer than 1023 bytes. Truncation is not itself memory-unsafe here (snprintf bounds and NUL-terminates), but it can change the trailing bytes of the name and, combined with basename(), yield a different or colliding filename than intended — a correctness/robustness concern, and a truncation that lands mid-multibyte could produce an odd but still single-quote-escaped name. The larger latent issue is basename() semantics: the XSI basename() may modify its input buffer and some implementations return a pointer to static storage, so 'base' must not be relied on across another basename() call. Within this function 'buf' outlives the use of 'base', so no dangling pointer occurs, but the contract is fragile if the code is refactored.

summary: A podcast-enclosure filename builder whose single-quote-to-%27 substitution (line 16) is a deliberate CWE-78 shell-injection mitigation; it is correct only under the unstated contract that the downstream shell command single-quotes the argument, and it escapes only the basename, not the download-path prefix. The one concrete local bug is an unsigned underflow on line 5: an empty download-path makes 'fn[fn.length()-1]' index std::string at SIZE_MAX, an out-of-bounds read / undefined behavior.

reviewed_anchors.reason: 'str' is the attacker-controlled enclosure URL entering from a remote feed and flowing toward a shell command; the trust boundary and the CWE-78 contract attach here.
Its return value seeds 'fn'; an empty return triggers the line-5 underflow, and its content is concatenated into the shell path WITHOUT escaping.
Unsigned underflow to SIZE_MAX when fn is empty => out-of-bounds std::string index (UB / OOB read).
A constant path-separator string ("/"); indexing [0] and appending it carries no attacker-controlled contract of its own.
Fixed buffer written only via bounded snprintf; no length field drives an unbounded copy into it, so no overflow contract.
Bounded, NUL-terminating, and uses a literal "%s" format (not CWE-134); only benign truncation. Noted in what_could_go_wrong for completeness, no standalone finding.
Return pointer stays valid within function scope; buf outlives its use and basename is called once, so no dangling/static-reuse bug materializes here.
Constant strftime format producing ~24 bytes into a 128-byte buffer; cannot overflow and contains no attacker data.
The sole shell-injection mitigation; its correctness depends on a downstream single-quoting contract and it does not escape the dlpath prefix.
Returns a value std::string by copy; no lifetime/dangling issue. The risk lives in how the caller uses it (captured by the line-16 finding).

findings.title: Unsigned underflow indexes std::string out of bounds when download-path is empty
findings.bug_class: Integer underflow leading to out-of-bounds read (std::string index)
findings.invariant: 'fn' must be verified non-empty (fn.empty() == false) before evaluating fn[fn.length()-1] / fn.back(); the last-character check must be guarded against a zero-length download directory.
findings.failure_mode: If ctrl->get_dlpath() returns "" (unset or misconfigured download-path), fn.length() is 0 and fn.length()-1 wraps to SIZE_MAX. fn[SIZE_MAX] invokes std::string::operator[] far past the end — undefined behavior and an out-of-bounds read that can crash the client or read adjacent memory. Triggered purely by configuration, on the very first line executed for every enclosure.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether get_dlpath() can ever return an empty string (default config, trimmed value) and whether any callers reach get_filename() before the path is validated non-empty.

findings.title: Shell-injection mitigation relies on an unstated single-quoting contract and skips the path prefix
findings.bug_class: OS command injection via incomplete/context-dependent shell escaping
findings.invariant: Every attacker/user-influenced component embedded in the shell command line must be escaped for the command's actual quoting context, and that context must genuinely be single-quoted; escaping only the single-quote character is safe ONLY inside a POSIX single-quoted string. The dlpath prefix must be escaped too if it can carry metacharacters.
findings.failure_mode: The returned path is later interpolated into a shell command run by podbeuter. Replacing only ' with %27 fully neutralizes a single-quoted context, but if the downstream command uses double quotes or no quotes, an enclosure URL with $(...), backticks, ;, |, &, or whitespace injects commands (remote code execution from a crafted feed). Separately, the dlpath prefix from get_dlpath() (line 3) is concatenated unescaped, so a download-path containing a single quote breaks out even when the basename is clean.
findings.what_to_confirm: Locate the call site that passes this filename to a shell (e.g. system()/popen()/exec of a shell string) and confirm the argument is wrapped in single quotes and that the full path — including the dlpath prefix — is escaped; verify %27 is never URL-decoded back to a quote before reaching the shell.

audit_checklist: Confirm ctrl->get_dlpath() cannot return an empty string, or add an fn.empty() guard before fn[fn.length()-1] on line 5.
Trace the returned filename to the exact shell invocation in podbeuter and verify the argument is enclosed in single quotes (the only context in which '->%27 escaping is sufficient).
Verify the download-path prefix (get_dlpath) is also escaped for the shell, since only 'base' is passed through replace_all.
Confirm the escaped filename is never percent-decoded back to a literal single quote before it reaches the shell.
Check that basename()'s return pointer is not cached across a second basename() call (XSI static-storage/buffer-mutation semantics).
Assess whether 1023-byte truncation of the enclosure name can cause filename collisions or unexpected paths downstream.
